metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `scripts/check_docs_index.py` pre-exists in the repo (cite the commit/PR that introduced it) OR add it to scope with an explicit 'create this script' task and remove the 'no code' constraint for that item.
evidence: Plan states script is 'wired into CI as the docs-index drift gate' — asserting pre-existence
Plan Scope section: 'No code behavior' — creating the script would violate stated scope
No S1–S6 story is cited as the origin of check_docs_index.py
Verify-command defect type 9: references a file with no dependency edge to the story that creates it
findings.finding: AC2's proving command (`python scripts/check_docs_index.py --check`) assumes `scripts/check_docs_index.py` already exists in the repo, but no prior story (S1–S6) is identified as creating it, and the plan's own scope explicitly excludes new code. If this script is not pre-existing infrastructure, AC2's oracle cannot run and the criterion is unverifiable in-session.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 (`docs/mcp-auth.md` linked from `docs/README.md`)
scenarios: Executor runs `python scripts/check_docs_index.py --check` and gets `No such file or directory`; AC2 cannot be closed
If executor creates the script to fix this, they violate the 'no code' scope constraint, creating an undeclared deliverable
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parenthetical in AC2: 'Note: this script was introduced in [PR/commit X]; verify it exists at that path before running.' If it does not pre-exist, add a scope bullet: 'Create `scripts/check_docs_index.py --check` that asserts every file under `docs/` is linked from `docs/README.md`' and update the Scope section accordingly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the ambiguous `make test / the manifest-check job` oracle in AC4 with a single unambiguous command (e.g. `make test && python scripts/check_server_manifest.py`) and add an explicit assertion that confirms new keys are present (e.g. `grep -q 'AUTH_RESOURCE_SERVER_URL' docs/env-vars.md`).
evidence: 'observable oracle: `make test` / the manifest-check job is green with all new keys present'
The '/' is not a shell operator here — it reads as 'make test OR the manifest-check CI job'
'with all new keys present' is not independently proven by `make test` alone unless the drift-gate script explicitly enumerates each new key
findings.finding: AC4's proving oracle is stated as `make test / the manifest-check job is green with all new keys present` — the slash makes it ambiguous whether the executor should run `make test`, check the CI job name, or both; and neither alone verifies 'all new keys present' as a distinct assertion.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 (`docs/mcp-reference.md` and `docs/env-vars.md` fully regenerated)
scenarios: Executor runs `make test`, it passes (because drift gates only compare against a stale baseline), but newly added keys from S6 are missing from the regenerated docs — AC4 is falsely closed
Executor is unsure whether to run make test locally or look at the CI job log

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Harden the ADR oracle to use `f=$(ls docs/adr/*mcp*auth*.md | head -1)` or specify the exact filename in the glob, so the grep command is deterministic when multiple matching files exist.
evidence: 'f=$(ls docs/adr/*mcp*auth*.md) && grep -Eq ... "$f"'
Verify-command defect: shell-expansion leakage when glob matches multiple paths
If a developer later adds a second ADR with a similar name (e.g. 'mcp-auth-v2'), the oracle breaks
findings.finding: The ADR oracle uses `f=$(ls docs/adr/*mcp*auth*.md)` — if the shell glob matches more than one file, `f` contains a newline-separated list of filenames, causing `grep ... "$f"` to attempt to open a file whose name is the multi-line string, silently failing with 'No such file or directory' rather than scanning the intended file.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 (ADR oracle command)
scenarios: Two ADR files match the glob; grep receives 'docs/adr/0042-mcp-auth.md\ndocs/adr/0043-mcp-auth-v2.md' as a filename argument and returns exit code 2
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `f=$(ls docs/adr/*mcp*auth*.md)` with `f=$(ls docs/adr/*mcp*auth*.md | head -1)` and add a preceding assertion `test $(ls docs/adr/*mcp*auth*.md | wc -l) -eq 1 || echo 'WARN: multiple ADR files matched'` to make the cardinality explicit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the ADR filename convention: either add an explicit naming requirement to the What/Scope section (e.g. 'filename must contain `mcp` and `auth`') or relax the AC 3 oracle to a broader glob or an explicit hardcoded filename.
evidence: Plan body: 'A new ADR under `docs/adr/` recording: ...' — no filename pattern specified
AC 3 oracle: `f=$(ls docs/adr/*mcp*auth*.md)` — requires both 'mcp' and 'auth' in the filename in that order
Contradiction: description is unconstrained on naming; criterion verify step is constrained to a specific glob
findings.finding: The ADR filename glob in AC 3's observable oracle (`ls docs/adr/*mcp*auth*.md`) is more restrictive than what the plan body describes ('A new ADR under `docs/adr/`' with no naming convention), creating a cross-section contradiction: a validly-named ADR (e.g. `docs/adr/0042-mcp-transport-security.md`) would satisfy the description but fail the oracle.
findings.location: AC 3 observable oracle vs. What section (ADR bullet)
scenarios: Implementer follows normal ADR numbering convention (e.g. `0012-mcp-transport-hardening.md`) and the oracle fails even though the deliverable is fully present
CI gate fails on a conformant file, causing confusion about what naming is actually required
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: 'Name the file `docs/adr/NNNN-mcp-auth.md`' and update the oracle to `f=docs/adr/*mcp*auth*.md` or simply `f=$(ls docs/adr/ | grep -i 'mcp.*auth\|auth.*mcp' | head -1); f=docs/adr/$f`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit grep to the AC 1 oracle for the residual-risks section heading (e.g. `grep -Eq 'Residual risk|authenticated.*authorized|per-tool' docs/mcp-auth.md`) or rename the described sub-bullet to match the oracle's `'## 7\. Explicitly out of scope'` heading so description and criterion are consistent.
evidence: What section: '**Residual risks (documented, not fixed here):** authenticated ≠ authorized — any authenticated principal...'
AC 1 oracle final check: `grep -Eq '^## 7\\. Explicitly out of scope' docs/mcp-auth.md`
No oracle line checks for a 'Residual risks' or 'Risks' heading; the described content could end up under any heading or mixed into 'Explicitly out of scope' contrary to reader expectation
findings.finding: AC 1's observable oracle checks for the heading `'^## 7\. Explicitly out of scope'` as the final section of `docs/mcp-auth.md`, but the What section describes a distinct 'Residual risks (documented, not fixed here)' section — these are different concepts, so the oracle can pass without a clearly labelled residual-risks section, or fail if an implementer creates both sections with correct headings but puts 'Explicitly out of scope' as section 8.
findings.location: AC 1 observable oracle (final grep) vs. What section (mcp-auth.md bullet, 'Residual risks' sub-bullet)
scenarios: Implementer creates `## 6. Residual risks` and `## 7. Explicitly out of scope` as separate sections — oracle passes but the residual risks section is unverified by the oracle

Implementer merges residual risks into section 7 to satisfy the oracle — but the What description implies it should be a distinct section covering authenticated≠authorized, rate-limiting delegation, etc.
findings.suggested_fix: Either add `grep -Eq 'Residual risk' docs/mcp-auth.md` to the oracle, or update the What section's sub-bullet to read 'Explicitly out of scope / residual risks' to align the description with the oracle heading.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a required section (e.g., `## 6. Troubleshooting`) to `docs/mcp-auth.md` covering: the shape of 401 responses and WWW-Authenticate headers, log signals for auth failures (rejected token, JWKS fetch error, introspection timeout), and a step-by-step debug checklist for the most common misconfigurations (wrong resource URL, JWKS URI unreachable, audience mismatch); update the AC grep oracle to assert this section exists.
evidence: AC grep oracle checks: '^## 1\\. Enabling', 'single audience', 'Behind a proxy', 'interactive-client', '^## 7\\. Explicitly out of scope' — no required heading for troubleshooting, error responses, or debug guidance

What section lists: 'residual risks', 'three interactive-client connection paths', 'behind-a-proxy deployment' — no mention of error-state documentation (rejected token UX, log output, WWW-Authenticate format)
ABSENCE: no AC line or What bullet requires documenting what operators observe when auth is misconfigured or tokens are rejected
findings.finding: The operator guide specification (docs/mcp-auth.md) covers configuration happy paths and residual risks but does not require a troubleshooting / error-state section: what an operator sees when a token is rejected (the WWW-Authenticate response, HTTP 401 body), how to debug a failed introspection or JWT validation, what log output indicates auth failure, or what to check when the PRM discovery URL returns wrong metadata. The AC grep oracle checks for section headings but none require a debugging/error-diagnosis section, leaving operators with no documented path to diagnose misconfiguration.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — first bullet (docs/mcp-auth.md content), grep oracle command
scenarios: Operator enables JWT verifier but JWKS URI is wrong — token validation silently fails; operator has no documented signals to diagnose (what does the 401 body say? what appears in logs?)
Operator sets auth_resource_server_url to http:// instead of https:// for a non-loopback bind — server rejects startup or tokens; operator needs to know where to look
Operator's IdP returns unexpected audience claim — composite verifier rejects; no documented 'how to confirm audience mismatch' troubleshooting step
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What bullet for docs/mcp-auth.md: '**Troubleshooting:** The shape of the 401/WWW-Authenticate response; log lines emitted on token rejection (JWKS fetch failure, audience mismatch, introspection timeout); a debug checklist (verify PRM URL, confirm resource claim, check JWKS reachability).' Add to the AC grep oracle: `grep -Eq '^## 6\\. Troubleshooting' docs/mcp-auth.md`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete prerequisite check to AC4 (or a new AC) that verifies at least one S1–S6 deliverable is present before S7's consistency check is considered valid — e.g., assert that `auth_strategies` or `auth_resource_server_url` appears in `docs/env-vars.md` before running the drift gate, so the gate cannot pass vacuously on a branch that hasn't merged S1–S6.
evidence: Plan: 'each key-adding story S1–S6 already registers ITS keys and regenerates its slice; S7 confirms the integrated whole is consistent'
grep for 'auth_strategies': no matches in codebase
grep for 'auth_resource_server_url': no matches in codebase (only one hit in a .rebar/cache plan-review artifact, not source)
grep for 'http_allowed_hosts': no matches
grep for 'http_allowed_origins': no matches
grep for 'required_scopes': no matches
grep for 'static verifier', 'jwt verifier', 'introspection', 'proxy verifier', 'custom verifier' (auth context): no matches in src/
grep for 'composite' (auth context) in src/: no matches
grep for 'WWW-Authenticate': only one cache hit, not in source
AC4 oracle: 'make test / the manifest-check job is green with all new keys present' — but if S1–S6 haven't been merged, there are no new keys and the gate trivially passes, giving a false green
findings.finding: The plan's 'Why' and 'What' sections assert that 'S1–S6 already registers ITS keys and regenerates its slice' and that S7 'confirms the integrated whole is consistent' — treating the S1–S6 auth implementation (five verifier modes, `auth_strategies` vocabulary, `auth_resource_server_url`, `http_allowed_hosts`, `http_allowed_origins`, `required_scopes`, composite audience choke point, etc.) as already shipped. A grep/read of the entire codebase finds zero matches for any of these auth config keys or verifier abstractions. The five verifier modes exist nowhere in `src/`. This means the plan rests on the premise that S1–S6 are merged before S7 executes — but that dependency is stated only in the 'Scope' section ('Depends on S1–S6') without any AC guard that verifies S1–S6 deliverables are actually present before S7 begins.
findings.location: Why section / Scope section / AC4 (drift-gate AC)
scenarios: S7 is started before S1–S6 are merged: the doc author has no shipped implementation to ground the guide against, so the guide may document the spec rather than actual behavior — creating drift between doc and code
AC4's 'all new keys present' oracle passes vacuously on the current branch (no new keys exist yet), giving false confidence that the drift gate is exercised
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC4: 'Prerequisite: `grep -q auth_resource_server_url docs/env-vars.md` exits 0 — confirming at least one S1–S6 auth key is present before the drift-gate consistency check is meaningful.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (by reading the S1–S6 implementation of `auth_resource_server_url` https-validation and PRM/`WWW-Authenticate` generation logic before finalizing the operator guide text) that the documented behavior matches the actual implementation.
evidence: Plan: '`auth_resource_server_url` MUST be the external https URL (it drives the served PRM `resource` and the `WWW-Authenticate: resource_metadata` URL — NOT the request scheme), and is validated https for non-loopback'
grep for 'auth_resource_server_url' in src/: zero matches
grep for 'resource_metadata' in src/: zero matches
grep for 'WWW-Authenticate' in src/: zero matches (one cache hit only)
findings.finding: The plan asserts `auth_resource_server_url` 'drives the served PRM `resource` and the `WWW-Authenticate: resource_metadata` URL — NOT the request scheme' and 'is validated https for non-loopback' as behavioral facts. No such config key or validation logic was found anywhere in the codebase. Since this claim drives the operator guide content and the behind-a-proxy deployment section, if S1–S6 implement this differently (e.g., derive the URL from the request scheme, or skip https validation on non-loopback), the doc would be wrong at publication.
findings.location: What section — 'Behind-a-proxy deployment' bullet; AC1 observable oracle (grep 'Behind a proxy')
scenarios: S1–S6 implement `auth_resource_server_url` with different behavior (e.g., https validation only on public IPs, not all non-loopback), causing the operator guide to give incorrect security guidance
The PRM endpoint path or `WWW-Authenticate` header format differs from what the doc describes, causing clients following the guide to fail

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact ADR filename (or at minimum the required slug pattern) in the plan body so it matches the AC3 oracle glob `*mcp*auth*` — e.g. `docs/adr/0050-mcp-auth.md` or `docs/adr/0050-mcp-authentication.md`.
evidence: Plan body: 'A new ADR under `docs/adr/`' — no filename or slug specified
AC3 oracle: `f=$(ls docs/adr/*mcp*auth*.md)` — glob requires 'mcp' before 'auth' in filename
Existing ADR naming pattern (e.g. 0049-opcert-asymmetric.md) shows slug is free-form, not derived from a fixed template
ABSENCE: no naming convention stated anywhere in the plan for the new ADR file
findings.finding: The ADR AC observable oracle uses the glob `docs/adr/*mcp*auth*.md` which requires the string 'mcp' to appear BEFORE 'auth' in the filename, but the plan body never specifies the ADR filename or naming convention. A plausible name like `0050-authenticated-mcp-transport.md` or `0050-auth-for-mcp.md` would NOT match the glob, silently causing the subsequent `grep` commands to operate on an empty expansion and fail with a shell error rather than a clear signal.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC 3 observable oracle: `f=$(ls docs/adr/*mcp*auth*.md)`
scenarios: Implementer names the ADR `0050-authenticated-mcp-transport.md` — glob matches, oracle passes.

Implementer names the ADR `0050-mcp-transport-security.md` — glob does NOT match (no 'auth' substring), oracle silently produces an empty `$f` and the grep exits non-zero with a misleading 'no such file' error.
Implementer names the ADR `0050-auth-hardening-for-mcp.md` — glob does NOT match ('auth' precedes 'mcp').
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan body under the ADR bullet: 'Name the ADR file `docs/adr/0050-mcp-auth.md`' (or the next available number containing both `mcp` and `auth` in that order to satisfy the AC oracle glob `docs/adr/*mcp*auth*.md`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit ADR filename convention to the plan (e.g. `docs/adr/NNNN-mcp-auth-resource-server.md`) so the AC3 oracle glob `*mcp*auth*` is guaranteed to match, or rewrite the oracle to use `ls docs/adr/0050-*.md` (or a path variable defined earlier in the AC).
evidence: Plan body: 'A new ADR under `docs/adr/`' — no filename pattern stated.
AC3 oracle: `f=$(ls docs/adr/*mcp*auth*.md) && grep -Eq ...`
Existing ADRs follow `NNNN-<slug>.md`; slug content is not constrained by the plan.
If the glob matches zero files, `ls` exits non-zero and the shell pipeline short-circuits before any grep runs, yielding a false-fail.
findings.finding: The AC3 observable oracle uses a glob `ls docs/adr/*mcp*auth*.md` that will fail (shell error or empty expansion) if the ADR file is named without both 'mcp' and 'auth' as substrings, yet the plan body specifies only 'A new ADR under docs/adr/' with no naming constraint. The existing ADR numbering is at 0049; an implementer naming the file `0050-mcp-transport-security.md` (missing 'auth') or `0050-authentication.md` (missing 'mcp') would satisfy the plan's body but break the oracle, making the AC silently unverifiable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 observable oracle: `f=$(ls docs/adr/*mcp*auth*.md)`
scenarios: Implementer names the ADR `0050-mcp-transport-security.md` — glob misses it, oracle fails despite correct content.
Implementer names it `0050-mcp-oauth-resource-server.md` — 'auth' is absent, oracle fails.
CI runs the oracle in a shell with `set -e`; the non-zero `ls` exit terminates the check before grep.
findings.suggested_fix: State the target filename in the plan (e.g. `docs/adr/0050-mcp-auth-resource-server.md`) and update the AC3 oracle to reference it directly: `f=docs/adr/0050-mcp-auth-resource-server.md && test -f "$f" && grep -Eq ...`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the env gate names in the plan's residual-risks bullet and ensure `docs/mcp-auth.md` uses the full canonical names: `REBAR_MCP_READONLY`, `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC`, and `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM`.
evidence: Plan text: '`REBAR_MCP_READONLY`/`ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC`/`ALLOW_LLM` env gates'
src/rebar/mcp_server.py line 9: 'REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC=1'
src/rebar/mcp_server.py line 11: 'REBAR_MCP_READONLY'
src/rebar/_mcp_writes.py line 293: 'Set REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM=1'
The plan abbreviates REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC → ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC and REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM → ALLOW_LLM.
findings.finding: The residual-risks section and AC1 content description refer to `REBAR_MCP_READONLY`/`ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC`/`ALLOW_LLM` env gates, but the actual environment variable names in the codebase are `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC` and `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM`. If the operator guide (`docs/mcp-auth.md`) reproduces the truncated forms, operators attempting to set these gates will use the wrong variable names and the gates will silently fail to activate.
findings.location: What section — residual risks bullet: '…gated only by server-level `required_scopes` and the existing `REBAR_MCP_READONLY`/`ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC`/`ALLOW_LLM` env gates'
scenarios: An operator following the guide sets `ALLOW_LLM=1` in the environment; `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM` is never set; the LLM gate silently remains inactive.
The operator guide is copy-pasted into a deployment script with the wrong variable names.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '`REBAR_MCP_READONLY`/`ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC`/`ALLOW_LLM`' with '`REBAR_MCP_READONLY` / `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC` / `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM`' throughout the plan and the resulting guide.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit `parent_id` to the S7 plan (or embed the parent epic's ticket URL/ID) and verify via `show_ticket(<parent_id>)` that every S7 deliverable — docs/mcp-auth.md, the ADR, the reference/manifest regeneration, and the docs-index CI gate — is within the parent epic's declared scope and does not contradict any parent AC.
evidence: Plan states 'Subset of the epic. Depends on S1–S6 (documents them).' but provides no explicit parent ticket ID.
All plausible parent ticket IDs (S7, MCP-AUTH, EPIC-MCP-AUTH, mcp-auth-epic, mcp-http-auth, http-auth, E1, EPIC1, mcp-auth-epic-2026) returned 'Ticket not found' from show_ticket.

G7 rubric requires: 'FETCH THE PARENT. The parent's id (parent_id) is provided in the ticket-graph context. Call show_ticket(<parent_id>) to read the parent's plan.' — the parent_id is absent from the plan text and not resolvable.
Without the parent's What/Scope/ACs, there is no authoritative contract to check S7's scope against.
findings.finding: The parent ticket (the epic containing S1–S7) cannot be fetched via `show_ticket` — all plausible IDs were tried and returned 'not found' — so the G7 leaf-parent containment check cannot be completed: it is impossible to verify that S7's declared scope (docs/mcp-auth.md, the ADR, reference/manifest consistency check) is a subset of the parent's contract and does not contradict any parent AC or success criterion.
findings.location: Plan header / Scope section — 'Depends on S1–S6 (documents them)'; no parent_id field present in plan
scenarios: S7 adds a docs-index CI drift gate (check_docs_index.py --check wired into CI) — if the parent epic did not include this gate, S7 is expanding scope beyond the parent's contract.
S7 positions itself as a 'final consistency check' coordinator over S1–S6 — if the parent scoped S7 as a pure documentation leaf with no coordinating/gate-wiring role, this is a scope divergence.
The ADR is scoped to document specific CVE/advisory identifiers (GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f, CVE-2026-40575, etc.) — if the parent did not include ADR authorship as a deliverable, this is out-of-scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before merging the ADR, verify that each cited advisory identifier (GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f, CVE-2026-40575, CVE-2026-27124, PVE-2026-93393) resolves to a real entry in its respective upstream registry (GitHub Security Advisories, NVD, or PyPI advisory DB); if any do not exist, replace with the correct identifier or remove the specific ID and retain only the vulnerability class + mitigation description.
evidence: Plan text: 'the specific advisory identifiers (GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f, CVE-2026-40575, CVE-2026-27124, PVE-2026-93393) are cited as the upstream-tracked references for those classes (as used by the mcp SDK / oauth2-proxy projects), NOT asserted here as independently NVD-verified facts'
Zero hits for any of these identifiers in the entire codebase (search_files confirms absence)
All four identifiers carry year-2026 identifiers — unusual for advisories that would already be cited in shipped software
The AC oracle only checks grep for 'DNS-rebinding', 'forwarded-header', etc. — it never validates the actual advisory IDs exist
findings.finding: The plan cites four specific security advisory identifiers (GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f, CVE-2026-40575, CVE-2026-27124, PVE-2026-93393) as 'upstream-tracked references' in the ADR, but these are future-dated 2026-era identifiers with zero presence in the codebase or any verifiable upstream registry citation in the plan. The AC hedges that they are 'NOT independently NVD-verified facts,' but it does NOT require verifying that these identifiers actually exist in any upstream advisory database (GHSA, NVD, PyPI advisory DB). If they are fabricated or mis-recalled identifiers, the ADR's security rationale becomes an unverifiable assertion — undermining rather than supporting the intended rigorous security review.
findings.location: AC line 3 (ADR observable oracle) / What section — ADR bullet citing GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f, CVE-2026-40575, CVE-2026-27124, PVE-2026-93393
scenarios: A security reviewer uses the ADR advisory citations to look up the referenced vulnerabilities and finds they don't exist in GHSA, NVD, or PyPI advisory DB — the ADR's security rationale collapses.
A future maintainer rotates to a newer mcp SDK version, searches for these CVEs to assess whether the class is still mitigated, and finds no matching advisory.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the ADR (and plan) whether 'standalone fastmcp' means the separate `fastmcp` PyPI package (distinct from the `mcp` SDK) or the `mcp.server.fastmcp` module, and provide a verifiable reference for the 'semver-exempt auth, CVE history' claim (e.g. a fastmcp changelog entry, GitHub issue, or release note).
evidence: Plan text: 'building our own verifiers on the official SDK seam vs adopting standalone `fastmcp` (semver-exempt auth, CVE history)'
Codebase: `src/rebar/mcp_server.py:243: from mcp.server.fastmcp import FastMCP` — FastMCP is already in use as part of the mcp SDK, not a separate package
scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py:72: from mcp.server.fastmcp import FastMCP — same SDK import
The 'semver-exempt auth' assertion about fastmcp has no supporting citation in the plan
findings.finding: The plan characterizes 'standalone fastmcp' as having 'semver-exempt auth, CVE history' to justify building own verifiers — but the installed codebase uses `from mcp.server.fastmcp import FastMCP` (i.e., FastMCP IS part of the official mcp SDK, not a separate package). If 'fastmcp' refers to a distinct standalone third-party package rather than the SDK's fastmcp module, the ADR must make this distinction unambiguous; if it refers to the SDK's own fastmcp, the 'semver-exempt auth' claim needs a verifiable reference since FastMCP is part of the same SDK the plan endorses building on.
findings.location: What section — ADR bullet: 'building our own verifiers on the official SDK seam vs adopting standalone fastmcp (semver-exempt auth, CVE history)'
scenarios: An ADR reader tries to verify the 'semver-exempt auth' claim about fastmcp and cannot determine whether this refers to the SDK module or a separate package, making the build-vs-adopt comparison unverifiable.
A maintainer sees the ADR endorsing 'the official SDK seam' while the code imports from the same SDK's fastmcp module, creating apparent contradiction.
findings.suggested_fix: If the intent is the standalone `fastmcp` package (pypi.org/project/fastmcp), clarify that and cite a specific release or issue. If it refers to the mcp SDK's own fastmcp, re-examine the claim since the project already depends on it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In docs/mcp-auth.md, qualify the Keycloak/Auth0/Okta DCR capability claims with minimum version requirements or configuration prerequisites, define 'CIMD' on first use (or replace with the standard term), and add a fallback note for when automatic DCR is disabled by IdP policy.
evidence: Plan text: '(2) automatic OAuth when the org IdP supports DCR or CIMD (Keycloak/Auth0/Okta can); (3) pre-registered --client-id/--client-secret for IdPs without anonymous DCR (Entra, Cognito)'
No verification of Keycloak/Auth0/Okta DCR support scope/version requirements anywhere in the plan or codebase
CIMD is not a widely-standardized term — its meaning in this context is unspecified
findings.finding: The operator guide specifies three interactive-client connection paths including 'automatic OAuth when the org IdP supports DCR or CIMD (Keycloak/Auth0/Okta can)' — but DCR (Dynamic Client Registration, RFC 7591) and CIMD support vary significantly across IdP versions and configurations. These are unverified capability assertions about third-party products that will appear as authoritative guidance in the operator guide. If an operator configures Entra (explicitly called out as requiring pre-registered clients) but their Keycloak version predates DCR support, the guide creates false expectations.
findings.location: What section — docs/mcp-auth.md spec: 'The three interactive-client connection paths … automatic OAuth when the org IdP supports DCR or CIMD (Keycloak/Auth0/Okta can)'
scenarios: An operator follows the guide expecting Okta to support automatic OAuth DCR, but their Okta org has anonymous DCR disabled by policy — they get an unhelpful error with no fallback guidance.
A reader interprets 'CIMD' as referring to a specific protocol but cannot find documentation for what it means in this MCP context.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Require that `docs/mcp-auth.md`'s `introspection` verifier section (and its flat-config example) explicitly documents that `introspection_client_secret` must be supplied via an environment variable or secrets manager — not stored as a plaintext value in `.rebar/config.conf` — and add a grep assertion for this guidance to the AC observable oracle (e.g. `grep -Eq 'introspection.*env|client_secret.*env' docs/mcp-auth.md`).
evidence: Plan specifies 'each of the five verifier modes (static, jwt, introspection, proxy, custom) with a flat-config example each' but does not require the introspection example to call out that client_id/client_secret must come from an env var or secrets manager rather than a plaintext config value.

The AC observable oracle grep checks only for heading presence (e.g. '^### `introspection`') and broad security-model phrases; it does not assert any secret-storage guidance for server-side credentials.
The 'tokens/secrets never logged' and '≥128-bit static token' requirements cover client-supplied tokens but not the introspection verifier's own outbound credential to the IdP.
ABSENCE: No plan phrase requires docs/mcp-auth.md to include guidance like 'set introspection_client_secret via environment variable; do not store in plaintext .conf'.
findings.finding: The operator guide spec (docs/mcp-auth.md) does not require documenting how the introspection verifier's own server-side client_secret (used to authenticate rebar to the IdP token introspection endpoint) should be stored — an operator following the flat-config example literally could place the credential in a plaintext config file on disk.
findings.location: What section / docs/mcp-auth.md AC — 'each of the five verifier modes … with a flat-config example each'; Acceptance Criteria bullet 1 observable oracle
scenarios: An operator deploys the introspection verifier, copies the flat-config example, and stores their IdP client_secret in .rebar/config.conf on disk — the credential is now in a world-readable file on the server and potentially in version control.
If the config file is committed to a repo (common in documentation-following ops), the introspection client_secret leaks into git history.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a mandatory note in the `introspection` verifier section of `docs/mcp-auth.md`: 'Never store `introspection_client_secret` as a plaintext config value. Supply it via an environment variable (e.g. `REBAR_MCP_INTROSPECTION_CLIENT_SECRET`) or a secrets manager; the config key accepts `${ENV_VAR}` interpolation.' Update the AC oracle to assert this guidance is present.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the plan (or cite the specific S1–S6 story) the normative interface contract for the `custom` verifier mode (e.g., Python callable signature, config field name for the dotted-path reference, and the concrete flat-config TOML example that S7 will document), so the documentation author has a defined schema to work from.
evidence: Plan text: 'each of the five verifier modes (`static`, `jwt`, `introspection`, `proxy`, `custom`) with a flat-config example each, and the closed `auth_strategies` vocabulary'
Plan text: 'State the flat-schema limitation: one instance per strategy type (for multiple issuers, use the `custom` verifier)' — but the custom verifier interface is never defined
Search for `auth_strategies` in codebase returns zero hits — vocabulary is entirely new from S1–S6, not cited in this plan
Search for `static jwt introspection proxy custom` returns zero hits — no pre-existing schema definition found
The plan contains no pointer to which S1–S6 deliverable defines the per-strategy field schema or the custom verifier callable contract
findings.finding: The `auth_strategies` closed vocabulary is referenced (five named modes: `static`, `jwt`, `introspection`, `proxy`, `custom`) and the plan requires 'a flat-config example each' be written in docs/mcp-auth.md, but neither this plan nor any cited prior document defines the per-strategy TOML key schema (what fields are required/optional under each strategy type). The `custom` verifier mode is the sharpest gap: the plan provides no definition of the protocol/interface a custom verifier must implement, so a documentation author cannot produce the promised flat-config example for it.
findings.location: What section — 'docs/mcp-auth.md' bullet, verifier modes paragraph; AC1 ('flat-config example each + the `auth_strategies` vocabulary')
scenarios: A documentation author works from this plan and can name the five verifier types but cannot write a correct flat-config TOML example for `custom` because the interface (Python dotted-path? URL? Callable signature?) is undefined here.
A reviewer checking AC1 ('flat-config example each') cannot evaluate correctness of the custom-verifier example without a normative spec.
An operator reading docs/mcp-auth.md may implement a `custom` verifier with the wrong interface because the documentation author had to guess.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief schema stub under the `docs/mcp-auth.md` bullet: e.g. 'The `custom` strategy accepts a `verifier_callable` dotted-path string pointing to a `Callable[[str], AuthResult]`; the flat-config example is: `[tool.rebar.mcp.auth_strategies.custom] verifier_callable = "mypackage.auth:verify_token"`.' Also add a sentence like 'Per-strategy field schemas are defined in S3 (jwt/introspection) and S4 (custom); S7 documents them as-shipped.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify (or cite the S1–S6 story that defines) how the one-instance-per-strategy-type limitation is enforced — e.g. 'raises `ConfigError` at load' vs. 'TOML last-wins silently' — so docs/mcp-auth.md can accurately describe the failure mode to operators.
evidence: Plan text: 'State the flat-schema limitation: **one instance per strategy type** (for multiple issuers, use the `custom` verifier)'
No pointer to which story (S1–S6) defines or enforces this constraint, and no description of how it is enforced (error vs. silent override vs. schema rejection)
The processing rule (limitation enforcement) is present in the documentation requirement but not co-located with any schema or code reference that defines its behavior
findings.finding: The 'one instance per strategy type' constraint is stated as a 'flat-schema limitation' that docs/mcp-auth.md must document, but no pointer is given to where this rule is enforced in the implementation (config validation, runtime startup, or schema). A documentation author writing the security model section cannot accurately describe the enforcement boundary (is it a ConfigError at load? a silent last-wins override? a startup assertion?) without a reference to where it is implemented.
findings.location: What section — 'docs/mcp-auth.md' bullet: 'State the flat-schema limitation: one instance per strategy type'
scenarios: Author documents the limitation as 'raises ConfigError' but it actually silently uses the last entry — the operator guide is wrong about failure mode.
Operators deploying multiple JWT issuers configure two `[tool.rebar.mcp.auth_strategies.jwt]` sections and receive unexpected behavior that docs don't accurately describe.

findings.finding: AC3 (ADR) has no observable oracle / proving command — only a prose checklist of required content. An executor cannot verify that all named decisions (composite audience choke point, CVE citations, deferred items) are actually present in the written ADR without a grep or script command.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'An ADR file exists under docs/adr/ recording…'
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC3: observable oracle: `ADR=$(ls docs/adr/*mcp-auth*.md | head -1) && grep -Eq 'resource-server-only' "$ADR" && grep -Eq 'composite.{0,20}audience|audience.{0,20}choke' "$ADR" && grep -Eq 'CVE|GHSA' "$ADR" && grep -Eq 'deferred' "$ADR"`

findings.finding: The ADR (as specified in the plan) is required to cite four specific security identifiers — GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f, CVE-2026-40575, CVE-2026-27124, and PVE-2026-93393 — as the motivating prior art for transport-hardening and composite-audience decisions, but none are verifiable: CVE-2026-* are future-dated identifiers with no NVD/MITRE records, PVE-2026-93393 does not match any recognized advisory namespace (NVD, GitHub Security Advisories, OSV, or PyPA), and repo search finds these strings only in prior review cache files — never in source or existing ADRs. An ADR meant for 'rigorous security review' that grounds architectural decisions in phantom identifiers fails the auditability purpose it declares.
findings.location: What section — ADR bullet: 'the transport hardening (citing GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f DNS-rebinding, oauth2-proxy CVE-2026-40575 forwarded-header, CVE-2026-27124 confused-deputy)' and 'the composite audience choke point (motivated by the SDK having no `.resource` backstop + the audience-confusion precedent PVE-2026-93393)'
findings.suggested_fix: For each identifier: (1) look up on nvd.nist.gov and github.com/advisories; (2) if found, use the canonical ID and link; (3) if the advisory doesn't exist yet, describe the threat class (e.g. 'DNS rebinding as described in RFC 6454 §7 and the WHATWG Fetch origin model') without claiming a specific CVE; (4) replace PVE-2026-93393 with either a real OSV/PyPA advisory URL or a prose statement of the audience-confusion threat class with a reference to RFC 8707 §2.

findings.finding: The AC1 grep oracle requires `grep -Eq 'out of scope' docs/mcp-auth.md` to prove the residual-risks section exists, but this is a pink-elephant/instruction-locality pattern: an executor can satisfy the oracle by placing the literal string 'out of scope' anywhere in the file (e.g., a footnote or unrelated paragraph) without actually authoring the required residual-risks content. All other section oracles in AC1 anchor to specific heading syntax ('^## ...', '^### `...`'), but 'out of scope' is a free-floating substring match with no heading anchor, making it the weakest oracle in the set.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC1 — observable oracle grep command: `grep -Eq 'out of scope' docs/mcp-auth.md`
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `grep -Eq 'out of scope' docs/mcp-auth.md` with `grep -Eq '^## .*[Rr]esidual [Rr]isk' docs/mcp-auth.md` (or whichever heading the plan intends, e.g., `'^## Residual risks'`), consistent with the anchored style used for all other section oracles in AC1.

findings.finding: The AC1 grep oracle uses `grep -Eq 'out of scope' docs/mcp-auth.md` to verify the residual-risks section, but 'out of scope' is a common phrase that can appear in unrelated contexts (e.g. the proxy or auth-strategy sections), creating a substring false-positive (VERIFY-COMMAND defect type 1) that would pass even if the residual-risks content is absent.
findings.location: AC1 proving command — `grep -Eq 'out of scope' docs/mcp-auth.md`
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `grep -Eq 'out of scope' docs/mcp-auth.md` with: `grep -Eq '^## .*[Rr]esidual' docs/mcp-auth.md && grep -Eq 'authenticated.{1,10}authorized' docs/mcp-auth.md && grep -Eq 'rate.lim' docs/mcp-auth.md && grep -Eq '128.bit\|static.token.entropy' docs/mcp-auth.md`

findings.finding: AC1's grep oracle does not verify the 'one-instance-per-strategy-type' limitation even though both the What section and the AC1 text explicitly require it to be documented, creating a gap between the stated requirement and its observable verification.
findings.location: AC1 (grep oracle vs. AC1 body text and What section)
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the AC1 shell oracle: `&& grep -Eq 'one instance per' docs/mcp-auth.md`

findings.finding: The AC1 oracle does not verify two content requirements explicitly listed in the 'What' section: (a) a concrete nginx/ALB header + allowlist example, and (b) that `auth_resource_server_url` drives the served PRM `resource` and `WWW-Authenticate` URL. These are substantive operator-safety requirements with no grep anchor.
findings.location: AC1 proving command vs. 'What' section bullets for behind-a-proxy deployment
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the AC1 oracle: `&& grep -Eq 'nginx|ALB' docs/mcp-auth.md && grep -Eq 'auth_resource_server_url' docs/mcp-auth.md && grep -Eq 'WWW-Authenticate' docs/mcp-auth.md`

findings.finding: The AC1 oracle greps for the literal string `'out of scope'` (`grep -Eq 'out of scope' docs/mcp-auth.md`) to verify the residual-risks section. However, the plan body consistently uses 'deferred' (e.g. 'per-tool scope→capability authorization is deferred', 'explicitly deferred items') — never 'out of scope'. If the author follows the plan's own vocabulary, the AC1 oracle will fail on a correctly-written document.
findings.location: AC1 oracle: `grep -Eq 'out of scope' docs/mcp-auth.md`; What section: 'per-tool scope→capability authorization is deferred'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the oracle to: `grep -Eq '(out of scope|deferred)' docs/mcp-auth.md`

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
